Math 102.formula: \lim_{x\to a}[f(x)\pm g(x)] = L\pm M,\quad \lim_{x\to a}[f(x)\cdot g(x)] = L\cdot M
Math 102.back: Sum, difference and product rules. Quotient rule: lim f/g = L/M if M ≠ 0. Constant multiple: lim[c·f(x)] = c·L. These laws let you break complicated limits into simpler parts.

Only for indeterminate forms 0/0 or ∞/∞. Differentiate numerator and denominator separately (not the quotient rule). May be applied repeatedly if the new limit is still indeterminate. Works for one‑sided limits and limits at infinity.
Memorise these – they appear frequently. The first is proved by squeezing. The second gives the derivative of aˣ. The third uses 1−cos x = 2 sin²(x/2) and the first limit.

Math 102.front: Three Conditions for Continuity at x = a
Math 102.back: All three must hold. Discontinuities: removable (hole) – condition (iii) fails; jump – left/right limits differ; infinite (vertical asymptote) – limit infinite or does not exist. Example: f(x)=1/x at 0 fails condition (i).
Chain rule: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x))·g'(x). Product: (uv)' = u'v + uv'. Quotient: (u/v)' = (u'v – uv')/v². These cover most elementary functions.
Definition of derivative – slope of tangent line. The limit must exist for differentiability. Differentiability implies continuity (but not vice versa). Use this definition to prove derivative rules.

Differentiate both sides of an equation with respect to x, treating y as a function of x. Every y‑term gets a dy/dx factor via chain rule. Collect dy/dx terms and solve. Example: x² + y² = 1 ⇒ 2x + 2y·y' = 0 ⇒ y' = –x/y.

Math 102.formula: Z_x = \frac{\partial Z}{\partial x}\bigg|_{y\text{ constant}},\quad Z_{xx}=\frac{\partial^2 Z}{\partial x^2},\quad Z_{xy}=\frac{\partial^2 Z}{\partial x\,\partial y}
Math 102.back: ∂Z/∂x: differentiate w.r.t. x, treat y as constant. Mixed partials Z_xy = Z_yx (Clairaut's theorem) if both are continuous. Used for multivariable functions, tangent planes, optimisation.

Math 102.formula: f'(c)=0\Rightarrow\text{critical point}.\quad f''(c)>0\Rightarrow\text{local min},\quad f''(c)<0\Rightarrow\text{local max},\quad f''(c)=0\Rightarrow\text{inconclusive (use sign analysis)}
Math 102.back: Set f'(x)=0 to find critical points. Use f'' to classify: positive ⇒ concave up ⇒ min; negative ⇒ concave down ⇒ max. If f''=0, check the sign of f' just left and right of c.
Steps: (1) domain, (2) intercepts (x=0, y=0), (3) symmetry (even/odd), (4) asymptotes (vertical, horizontal, oblique), (5) critical points from y'=0, (6) concavity from y''=0, (7) sketch.
m_T = dy/dx evaluated at the point. For implicit curves, use implicit differentiation to get m_T. Normal is perpendicular, so its slope is the negative reciprocal (provided m_T ≠ 0).
For x=f(t), y=g(t): slope = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt). Second derivative uses the same denominator dx/dt. Horizontal tangent when dy/dt=0 but dx/dt≠0; vertical tangent when dx/dt=0 but dy/dt≠0.
Also: ∫sin x dx = –cos x + C, ∫cos x dx = sin x + C, ∫sec²x dx = tan x + C, ∫sec x tan x dx = sec x + C, ∫csc²x dx = –cot x + C. Always add constant C for indefinite integrals.
LIATE: Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential – choose u as whichever comes first. Example: ∫x eˣ dx: u=x (Algebraic), dv=eˣ dx ⇒ du=dx, v=eˣ ⇒ result = x eˣ – eˣ + C.
Swap x and y, then solve for y. The graph of f⁻¹ is the reflection of f across y = x. f must be one‑to‑one (horizontal line test). Domain of f⁻¹ = range of f.

Math 102.formula: \text{If } g(x) \le f(x) \le h(x) \text{ near } a \text{ and } \lim_{x\to a}g(x)=\lim_{x\to a}h(x)=L,\text{ then } \lim_{x\to a}f(x)=L
Math 102.back: Useful for limits like x² sin(1/x) as x→0. Because –1 ≤ sin(1/x) ≤ 1 ⇒ –x² ≤ x² sin(1/x) ≤ x², and both bounds → 0.
Vertical asymptote at x=a if the one‑sided limit is infinite. For rational functions, check where denominator = 0 but numerator ≠ 0. Example: f(x)=1/(x–2) has a VA at x=2.
For rational functions: compare degrees. If degree(num) < degree(denom), limit = 0. If equal, limit = ratio of leading coefficients. If greater, limit = ±∞ (no HA; may have oblique asymptote).

Math 102.formula: \text{If } f \text{ continuous on }[a,b] \text{ and } y_0 \text{ between } f(a) \text{ and } f(b), \exists c\in(a,b): f(c)=y_0
Math 102.back: Used to prove existence of roots. If f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs, there is at least one root in (a,b). Example: f(x)=x³–x–1 on [1,2]: f(1)=–1, f(2)=5 ⇒ root between 1 and 2.
Memorise these. Domain restrictions: sin⁻¹ x defined for |x|≤1, tan⁻¹ x all reals, sec⁻¹ x |x|≥1. Derivatives of cos⁻¹ and cot⁻¹ are negatives of the above.
Used when function has products, quotients, or variable exponents (e.g., y = xˣ). Differentiate both sides, then solve for y'. Example: y = xˣ ⇒ ln y = x ln x ⇒ y'/y = ln x + 1 ⇒ y' = xˣ(ln x + 1).

Math 102.formula: \text{If } f \text{ continuous on }[a,b] \text{ and differentiable on }(a,b), \exists c\in(a,b): f'(c)=\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}
Math 102.back: Geometrically: there is a point where the tangent line is parallel to the secant line. Special case: Rolle’s theorem (f(a)=f(b) ⇒ f'(c)=0). Used to prove inequalities and properties of functions.

Math 102.formula: f(x) \approx f(a) + f'(a)(x-a),\quad dy = f'(x) dx
Math 102.back: Estimates function values near a known point. Error is O((x–a)²). For small Δx, Δf ≈ f'(a)Δx. Example: √4.1 ≈ 2 + (1/(2·2))·0.1 = 2.025.
Iterative root‑finding. Requires good initial guess. Fails if f'(x_n)=0 or if function is not well‑behaved. Quadratic convergence near a simple root. Example: find √2 using f(x)=x²–2.

Math 102.formula: \text{(1) } \frac{d}{dx}\int_a^x f(t) dt = f(x),\quad \text{(2) } \int_a^b f(x) dx = F(b)-F(a)
Math 102.back: Part 1: derivative of the accumulation function returns the integrand. Part 2: evaluate antiderivative at bounds. Connects differentiation and integration. F is any antiderivative of f.

Math 102.formula: \int f(g(x)) g'(x) dx = \int f(u) du,\quad u=g(x)
Math 102.back: Reverse of chain rule. For definite integrals, change limits: ∫ₐᵇ f(g(x))g'(x)dx = ∫_{g(a)}^{g(b)} f(u) du. Example: ∫ 2x cos(x²) dx, let u=x² ⇒ du=2x dx ⇒ ∫ cos u du = sin u + C = sin(x²)+C.

Math 102.formula: \int \sin^m x \cos^n x dx,\quad \int \tan^m x \sec^n x dx
Math 102.back: Strategy: if m odd (sin power), save one sin, use sin²=1–cos², substitute u=cos x. If n odd (cos power), save one cos, use cos²=1–sin², substitute u=sin x. If both even, use half‑angle formulas sin²x = (1–cos2x)/2, cos²x=(1+cos2x)/2.
Eliminates square roots. After integration, convert back using a right triangle. Example: ∫√(1–x²)dx, x=sinθ ⇒ √(1–x²)=cosθ, dx=cosθ dθ ⇒ ∫cos²θ dθ = (θ+sinθ cosθ)/2 + C = (sin⁻¹x + x√(1–x²))/2 + C.
Requires deg(P) < deg(Q). Factor Q into linear and irreducible quadratics. Solve for constants by clearing denominators and equating coefficients or substituting convenient x‑values.

Math 102.formula: \int_a^\infty f(x)dx = \lim_{b\to\infty} \int_a^b f(x)dx,\quad \int_a^b f(x)dx \text{ with singularity at } a \text{ or } b
Math 102.back: Converges if the limit exists finitely. Compare with p‑integrals: ∫₁^∞ 1/xᵖ dx converges for p>1, diverges for p≤1. ∫₀¹ 1/xᵖ dx converges for p<1, diverges p≥1.
Separate variables, integrate both sides. Do not forget the constant C. An initial condition gives a particular solution. Example: dy/dx = x/y ⇒ y dy = x dx ⇒ y²/2 = x²/2 + C ⇒ y² – x² = constant.

Math 102.formula: \frac{dy}{dx} + P(x)y = Q(x),\quad \mu(x)=e^{\int P dx},\quad \frac{d}{dx}(\mu y)=\mu Q
Math 102.back: Integrating factor method. Multiply by μ, the left side becomes d(μy)/dx, then integrate. Example: dy/dx – 2y = eˣ, μ=e^{–2x}, d(ye^{–2x})/dx = e^{–x}, solution y = –eˣ + Ce^{2x}.
If the limit exists (finite), the sequence converges; otherwise it diverges. Squeeze theorem also applies to sequences. Example: a_n = 1/n → 0.
Partial sum: S_N = a(1–r^{N+1})/(1–r). For |r|<1, r^{N+1}→0, so S_N→a/(1–r). If r=1, series diverges (unless a=0).

Math 102.formula: \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^p} \text{ converges if } p>1, \text{ diverges if } p \le 1
Math 102.back: p=1 gives harmonic series (diverges). p=2 converges to π²/6. Test via integral test: ∫₁^∞ x^{-p} dx converges iff p>1.

Math 102.formula: 0 ≤ a_n ≤ b_n: ∑b_n conv ⇒ ∑a_n conv. ∑a_n div ⇒ ∑b_n div. \quad \lim a_n/b_n = c∈(0,∞) ⇒ same behavior
Math 102.back: Limit comparison is often easier. Choose b_n as a p‑series or geometric series. Example: ∑ 1/(n²+1) compare to 1/n².
Works well for series with factorials or exponentials. Example: ∑ n!/nⁿ, ratio = (n+1)!/(n+1)^{n+1} · nⁿ/n! = (n/(n+1))ⁿ → 1/e < 1 ⇒ converges.
Useful when terms involve n‑th powers. Example: ∑ (2n/(3n+1))ⁿ, nth root = 2n/(3n+1) → 2/3 < 1 ⇒ converges.

Math 102.formula: \text{If } b_n \text{ decreasing, } \lim b_n=0, \text{ then } \sum (-1)^n b_n \text{ converges (conditionally)}
Math 102.back: Example: alternating harmonic series ∑ (–1)ⁿ⁻¹/n converges conditionally. Error bound: |remainder| ≤ first omitted term.
Series ∑ c_n (x−a)ⁿ converges for |x−a|<R, diverges for |x−a|>R. At endpoints x=a±R, test separately (e.g., alternating series test, p‑series).
Common expansions: eˣ = ∑ xⁿ/n!, sin x = ∑ (–1)ⁿ x²ⁿ⁺¹/(2n+1)!, cos x = ∑ (–1)ⁿ x²ⁿ/(2n)!, 1/(1−x)=∑ xⁿ for |x|<1.

Math 102.formula: |R_n(x)| \le \frac{M}{(n+1)!}|x-a|^{n+1}, \text{ where } |f^{(n+1)}(z)| \le M \text{ for } z \text{ between } a \text{ and } x
Math 102.back: Estimates the error when using an n‑th degree Taylor polynomial. For alternating series, error ≤ first omitted term, often easier.

Math 102.formula: \frac{\partial z}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt},\quad \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x}\frac{\partial x}{\partial s} + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}\frac{\partial y}{\partial s}
Math 102.back: For z = f(x,y) with x = g(s,t), y = h(s,t). Draw a tree diagram to remember all paths. Used in multivariable optimisation and physics.
Points in direction of greatest increase of f. Magnitude = directional derivative in that direction. ∇f is perpendicular to level curves (or level surfaces).
Rate of change of f in direction u. Maximum when u aligns with ∇f (θ=0), minimum opposite (θ=π). If ∇f = 0, directional derivative is zero in all directions.
Find extrema of f subject to constraint g=c. Solve ∇f = λ∇g together with g=k. λ is the Lagrange multiplier (scales the gradients). For three variables, add a third equation.

Math 102.formula: \iint_R f(x,y) dA = \int_{x=a}^b \int_{y=c}^d f(x,y) dy dx = \int_{y=c}^d \int_{x=a}^b f(x,y) dx dy
Math 102.back: Fubini’s theorem: iterated integrals (order can be swapped if f is continuous). The area of region R = ∬_R dA.

Math 102.formula: \iint_R f(x,y) dA = \int_{\theta=\alpha}^{\beta} \int_{r=r_1(\theta)}^{r_2(\theta)} f(r\cos\theta, r\sin\theta) \, r \, dr d\theta
Math 102.back: Jacobian factor = r. Useful for circular regions. Limits: r ≥ 0, θ from 0 to 2π for full circle. Example: ∫∫ e^{-x²–y²} dA = ∫₀^{2π}∫₀^∞ e^{-r²} r dr dθ = π.

Math 102.formula: \oint_C (L dx + M dy) = \iint_R \left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial L}{\partial y}\right) dA
Math 102.back: Relates line integral around simple closed curve C to double integral over region R. Requires L,M have continuous partials. Orientation: positive (counter‑clockwise).

Math 102.formula: \nabla\cdot\mathbf{F} = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}
Math 102.back: Measures net outflow per unit volume. Positive divergence = source, negative = sink. Divergence theorem relates flux through closed surface to volume integral of divergence.
Measures rotation at a point. If curl = 0, the field is conservative (F = ∇φ). Used in Stokes’ theorem to relate line integral to surface integral of curl.
Relates line integral around boundary curve C to surface integral of curl over oriented surface S. Orientation: right‑hand rule. Generalises Green’s theorem to 3D.
Converts flux through closed surface to volume integral of divergence. E is solid region, ∂E its boundary. Used in electromagnetism (Gauss’s law).

MTH 103.back: θ measured from positive x‑axis. If the angle is from the y‑axis, swap sin and cos. Always draw a diagram to determine correct quadrant signs. Components add as scalars.
Unit vector has magnitude exactly 1. To make any vector into a unit vector, divide each component by the magnitude. Used to describe direction without scaling.
α, β, γ are angles the vector makes with x, y, z axes. The identity provides a useful check. The unit vector = ⟨cosα, cosβ, cosγ⟩.
Scalar projection gives signed length of the shadow (can be negative). Vector projection gives a vector parallel to b. Work done by a force: W = F·d = |F| (scalar proj of F onto d) × |d|.
Commutative: a·b = b·a. If a·b = 0 and a,b ≠ 0, vectors are perpendicular. Self‑dot: a·a = |a|². Use to find the angle between vectors: cosθ = (a·b)/(|a||b|).
Result is perpendicular to both a and b. Direction given by right‑hand rule. Anti‑commutative: a×b = –(b×a). If a×b = 0, vectors are parallel. |a×b| = area of parallelogram.
a is a known point on the line, d is direction vector, t parameter. Cartesian form (symmetric equations) on the right. Direction vector from two points: d = B – A.

Position vector of point P is from origin O to P. Vector from A to B = b – a (tip minus tail). Midpoint = average of position vectors. Section formula: dividing AB in ratio m:n internally = (n a + m b)/(m+n).
For parallel: a × b = 0, or a = λb for some scalar λ (component ratios equal). Both conditions are frequently tested.
Add component‑wise. Graphically: parallelogram law for addition, tail‑to‑tip for subtraction. Vector a – b gives displacement from b to a.
Changes magnitude by factor |c|. If c>0, direction unchanged; if c<0, direction reversed. Used to scale vectors. Example: 2v doubles the length.
Express a vector as sum of scaled basis vectors. In ℝ², any vector can be written as combination of two non‑parallel vectors (basis). The scalars are coordinates.
Any vector in ℝ³ = a i + b j + c k. Orthonormal: i·i=j·j=k·k=1, i·j=0, etc. Right‑handed: i×j = k.

MTH 103.formula: \mathbf{a}\cdot(\mathbf{b}\times\mathbf{c}) = \begin{vmatrix} a_x & a_y & a_z \\ b_x & b_y & b_z \\ c_x & c_y & c_z \end{vmatrix}
MTH 103.back: Absolute value = volume of parallelepiped spanned by a,b,c. Cyclic permutation does not change value: a·(b×c)=b·(c×a)=c·(a×b). If zero, vectors are coplanar.
BAC‑CAB rule. Result lies in plane of b and c. Useful in physics (angular momentum, Lorentz force).
n = ⟨a,b,c⟩ is normal vector. Given three points, find two vectors in the plane, cross product gives n, then use one point to get equation.
Absolute value gives distance. Sign indicates which side of the plane. Point lies on plane if numerator = 0.

MTH 103.formula: D = \frac{|\overrightarrow{P_0P} \times \mathbf{d}|}{|\mathbf{d}|}, \text{ where } \mathbf{d} \text{ is direction vector of line}
MTH 103.back: P₀ is point on line, P is external point. Cross product magnitude = area of parallelogram, dividing by |d| gives height = distance.
Angle between normals gives acute angle between planes. If n₁·n₂ = 0, planes are perpendicular. If we want the dihedral angle, take the acute one.

MTH 103.formula: \text{Solve the two plane equations simultaneously. Direction vector } = \mathbf{n}_1\times\mathbf{n}_2
MTH 103.back: If normals are not parallel, planes intersect in a line. Find one point (set one variable = 0) and direction = cross product of normals.
Real part Re(z)=x, imaginary part Im(z)=y. Multiplication: (a+ib)(c+id)= (ac–bd) + i(ad+bc). Division: multiply numerator and denominator by conjugate.
Conjugate is reflection across real axis. Used to divide complex numbers: (a+ib)/(c+id) = (a+ib)(c−id)/(c²+d²). Also gives modulus squared.
Polar form: z = |z|(cosθ + i sinθ). Argument not unique: add 2πk. For z=0, argument undefined. Principal argument Arg(z) is usually taken in (–π,π] or [0,2π).
Euler's formula: connects exponentials and trig functions. Multiplication: r₁e^{iθ₁}·r₂e^{iθ₂}=r₁r₂e^{i(θ₁+θ₂)}. Division: (r₁/r₂) e^{i(θ₁–θ₂)}.

MTH 103.formula: (r e^{i\theta})^n = r^n e^{i n\theta} = r^n(\cos n\theta + i\sin n\theta)
MTH 103.back: Used for powers and roots of complex numbers. Roots: z^{1/n} = r^{1/n} e^{i(θ+2πk)/n}, k=0,1,...,n−1. Gives n distinct roots equally spaced on a circle.

MTH 103.formula: z^n = 1 \Rightarrow z = e^{2\pi i k/n} = \cos\frac{2\pi k}{n} + i\sin\frac{2\pi k}{n}, \quad k=0,1,\dots,n-1
MTH 103.back: Sum of all roots = 0. Product = (−1)^{n−1}. Geometric: vertices of regular n‑gon on unit circle. Used in discrete Fourier transform.
Multi‑valued function. Principal value uses Arg(z) ∈ (–π,π]. Used to solve exponential equations. For real x, ln(–1) = iπ (principal) + 2πik.

MTH 103.formula: z^c = e^{c\ln z} \text{ (multi‑valued unless c integer)}
MTH 103.back: Example: iⁱ = e^{i·ln i} = e^{i·(iπ/2+2πik)} = e^{-π/2 - 2πk}. Infinitely many real values (take k such that expression real).
Scalar projection of force onto displacement times magnitude of displacement. Unit: Joule (N·m). Zero if force ⟂ displacement.
r = position vector from pivot to point of application. Magnitude = rF sinθ. Direction: right‑hand rule. Unit: N·m.
For points A,B,C. Cross product magnitude gives area of parallelogram; half for triangle. In 2D: ½|(x₂–x₁)(y₃–y₁)–(x₃–x₁)(y₂–y₁)|.
Absolute value of scalar triple product. a,b,c are edges from one vertex. Zero if vectors are coplanar.
Addition requires same dimensions. Scalar multiplication multiplies each entry. Both are commutative and associative.
Columns of A must equal rows of B. Not commutative (AB ≠ BA generally). Identity I such that AI = IA = A.
Flips rows and columns. Symmetric if A^T = A. Skew‑symmetric if A^T = –A. Used in dot products: a·b = aᵀb.
Det ≠ 0 iff matrix invertible. Represents scaling factor of area/volume. For 3×3, use Sarrus rule or expansion by minors.
Exists only if ad–bc ≠ 0. For larger matrices: use Gauss‑Jordan elimination or adjugate. A A⁻¹ = I.

MTH 103.formula: x_i = \frac{\det(A_i)}{\det(A)},\quad A_i \text{ replaces column } i \text{ with RHS}
MTH 103.back: Works only for square systems with det(A)≠0. Not efficient for large n but good for 2×2, 3×3. Example: 2×2 gives x = (ed–bf)/(ad–bc).
λ from characteristic polynomial. For each λ, solve (A–λI)v=0 to find eigenvectors. Used in diagonalisation, quantum mechanics.
Sum of diagonal entries equals sum of eigenvalues (with multiplicities). tr(AB)=tr(BA). Cyclic property: tr(ABC)=tr(BCA).

MTH 103.formula: A = PDP^{-1} \text{ where } D \text{ diagonal, columns of } P \text{ are eigenvectors}
MTH 103.back: Requires n linearly independent eigenvectors. Then Aᵏ = PDᵏP⁻¹, useful for powers and exponentials. Not all matrices are diagonalisable.
Produces orthogonal basis from linearly independent set. Normalise to get orthonormal basis. Example: from {(1,0),(1,1)} get {(1,0),(0,1)}.
Best approximation in least squares sense. Residual v – proj_W v is orthogonal to W. Used in regression (normal equations).
For coordinate axes: i×j = k, j×k = i, k×i = j. Cyclic order. Switching order changes sign.

MTH 103.formula: V(t) = V_0 e^{i(\omega t + \phi)} \Rightarrow \text{Impedance: } Z = R + iX
MTH 103.back: Real part gives actual voltage/current. Complex impedance combines resistance R and reactance X. Ohm’s law: V = IZ.
Phasor = A e^{iφ}. Addition of waves becomes addition of complex amplitudes, greatly simplifying interference problems.

GST 112.back: Iwo‑Eleru rock shelter (Ondo State) – ~11,000 years ago. Nok culture (c. 500 BC–200 AD) – famous for terracotta figurines and earliest iron‑smelting in West Africa.
Three dominant groups: Hausa‑Fulani (North), Yoruba (Southwest), Igbo (Southeast). Nigeria has 250+ ethnic groups. South‑East is least ethnically diverse (predominantly Igbo).

Kanem‑Borno (Kanuri people, N‑East) – one of longest‑lasting African empires. Oyo Empire (Yoruba) – cavalry‑based. Benin Kingdom – advanced bronze casting. Sokoto Caliphate – founded 1804 by Usman dan Fodio.

Islam entered via trans‑Saharan trade routes by 11th century. Usman dan Fodio led a jihad in 1804, founding the Sokoto Caliphate – largest pre‑colonial state in West Africa. Spread to Hausa city‑states.

Christianity spread mainly in the South. CMS (Church Missionary Society) arrived 1840s. Samuel Ajayi Crowther – first African Anglican bishop, 1864. Mission schools laid foundation for Western education.

1861: Lagos annexed by Britain. 1900: Protectorates of Northern and Southern Nigeria created. 1914: Amalgamation of North and South by Lord Lugard. 1960: Independence (Oct 1). 1963: Republic declared.

Policy introduced by Lord Lugard – British ruled through existing traditional rulers (emirs, chiefs). Worked well in the North (strong traditional authority). Less effective in Southeast (no centralised chiefs – ‘Warrant Chiefs’ were artificial).

1967–1970. Cause: Igbo secession following 1966 coups and ethnic violence. Biafra declared by Ojukwu; Federal govt led by Gowon. Ended Jan 1970 with ‘No victor, no vanquished’ declaration. ~1–3 million deaths (mostly starvation).

ICPC (Independent Corrupt Practices Commission) – est. 2000 under Obasanjo. EFCC (Economic and Financial Crimes Commission) – est. 2003. Both anti‑corruption bodies. Key legislation: Criminal Code (South), Penal Code (North).

Also called Ogu Umunwanyi (Women’s War). Igbo and Ibibio women protested against potential taxation and changes to indirect rule. Thousands marched; British forces killed ~50 women. Led to reforms in Native Administration.
Terracotta sculptures, iron smelting, spread across present‑day Kaduna, Kano, Plateau states. One of the earliest complex societies in West Africa south of the Sahara.
Located around Lake Chad. Sao (c. 500 BC – 1500 AD) known for clay figurines and copper alloys. Daima existed from 500 BC to 1500 AD.
From south to north: Mangrove swamp, Rainforest, Guinea savanna, Sudan savanna, Sahel (semi‑desert). Niger and Benue rivers are the two main rivers.
Oil – Niger Delta, Imo, Abia, Rivers, Bayelsa, Delta. Tin – Plateau. Coal – Enugu, Benue. Limestone – Ogun, Kogi, Cross River. Gold – Zamfara, Osun, Niger.
Ife (religious/cultural centre), Oyo (military empire), Ijebu, Egba, Ondo, Ilesha. Each had its own Oba. Ife considered origin by tradition (Oduduwa).
Segmentary lineage system. No centralised monarchy; decisions made by councils of elders (ama ala) and age grades. Title of ‘Eze’ became more common after colonial interference.
Capital: Benin City (Edo). Famous for bronze plaques and ivory carvings (16th–17th c). Oba Ewuare the Great (1440–1473) expanded kingdom. Contact with Portuguese (1485).
Centred around Lake Chad. Rulers: Mai (king). Converted to Islam by 11th century. Invaded by Bulala in 14th c, moved to Borno. Peak under Mai Idris Alooma (1571–1603) – built walls, used firearms.
Seven true states: Daura, Kano, Katsina, Zazzau (Zaria), Gobir, Rano, Biram. Seven vassal states (Zamfara, Kebbi, etc). Gobir and Kano most powerful. Conquered by Fulani jihad 1804–1810.
Kanem‑Borno had strong Islamic scholarship. Sheikh al‑Maghili visited Hausaland (~1490). Universities of Sankore (Timbuktu) influenced Hausa scholars.
Founded by Usman dan Fodio after jihad. Capital at Sokoto, with emirates each ruled by an emir paying allegiance to the Sultan. Legal system based on Maliki school of Islamic law.
Catholic missionaries: Portuguese in Benin (1500s) but no long‑term impact. CMS (Anglican) arrived 1842 under Henry Townsend. Methodists arrived 1842. Roman Catholic missions reappeared 1860s.

Missionaries established schools teaching English, reading, writing, arithmetic. First grammar school: CMS Grammar School, Lagos (1859). Yoruba language first written with Latin script due to missionary work.
European powers partitioned Africa. Britain gained claim to the area that became Nigeria. Led to Royal Niger Company (1886) under George Goldie.
Chartered company that governed Niger area until 1900. Imposed treaties and trade monopolies. Overthrew King Jaja of Opobo (1887) and Nana of Itsekiri.
Lord Lugard unified Northern and Southern Protectorates plus Colony of Lagos. Named ‘Nigeria’ (from Niger). Rationale: administrative convenience and economic viability.
First Governor‑General of Nigeria (1914–1919). Architect of Indirect Rule. Also served in East Africa. Wrote ‘The Dual Mandate in British Tropical Africa’ (1922).
Herbert Macaulay (NNDP, 1923) – first political party. NCNC (1936, Azikiwe). Action Group (1951, Awolowo). Northern People’s Congress (1949, Ahmadu Bello).
Clifford Constitution (1922): elective principle. Richards (1946): regional assemblies. Macpherson (1951): central and regional houses. Lyttleton (1954): true federalism.
October 1, 1960. Governor‑General (Nigerian) as Queen’s representative. Prime Minister: Tafawa Balewa. President of Senate: Nnamdi Azikiwe. Leader of Opposition: Awolowo.
Nigeria became a republic within Commonwealth. President (Azikiwe) as head of state. Still retained Westminster parliamentary system. Regions: North, East, West, Mid‑West (created 1963).
January 15, 1966: first military coup, mostly Igbo officers (Ironsi). July 29, 1966: counter‑coup, mostly Northern officers (Gowon). Led to pogroms against Igbos in North.
May 30, 1967: Lt. Col. Emeka Ojukwu declared independence of Eastern Region renamed Biafra. Federal government declared war July 6, 1967.
1967: Nigerian forces capture Enugu (Oct), Calabar (Nov). 1968: Port Harcourt falls (May). 1969‑70: starvation blockade. Biafra surrendered on January 15, 1970.
Reconstruction, Rehabilitation, Reconciliation. 12 new states created after war. Indigenisation decree (1972) to transfer economic control to Nigerians.
Murtala Muhammed assassinated 1976 (failed coup). Obasanjo completed transition. New capital Abuja proposed. Led to Second Republic (1979‑83).
US‑style presidential system. Executive president, bicameral legislature (Senate, House of Representatives). President Shehu Shagari (NPN).
Presidential election annulled (MKO Abiola vs Bashir Tofa). Interim government (Ernest Shonekan). Abacha coup Nov 1993. Abiola died in custody 1998.
Olusegun Obasanjo (1999‑2007), Umaru Yar’Adua (2007‑10), Goodluck Jonathan (2010‑15), Muhammadu Buhari (2015‑23), Bola Tinubu (2023‑present).
Three branches: Executive (President, State Governors, Ministers), Legislature (National Assembly: Senate 109, House 360), Judiciary (Supreme Court, Court of Appeal, Federal High Court).
774 Local Government Areas. Each with elected Chairman and Councillors. Revenue: statutory allocation from Federation Account, internally generated revenue.
APC (All Progressives Congress) – centre‑right. PDP (People’s Democratic Party) – centre‑left. LP (Labour Party), NNPP, SDP.
Revenue from oil, taxes, customs shared among federal (52.68%), states (26.72%), local governments (20.60%). Plus derivation principle (13% of oil revenue to oil‑producing states).
Oil & gas (about 65% of govt revenue, >90% of export earnings). Agriculture: rubber, cocoa, oil palm, groundnuts, cassava. Services: telecoms, banking, Nollywood.
Halliburton (bribes for LNG contracts), Pension fraud (₦100bn), Malabu oil deal (₦1.7bn), Arms procurement scandal (Dasan).
16th‑century Hausa warrior queen. Expanded territory, built defensive walls (Zazzau, now Zaria). Subject of many legends.
Women’s rights activist, first Nigerian woman to drive a car. Led Abeokuta Women’s Union against taxation (1940s). Mother of Fela Kuti.
Surveyed education in British West Africa. Recommended adaptation of curricula to local needs, expansion of vocational education.
Led to establishment of universities in Lagos (1962), Ife (1962), Ahmadu Bello (1962), Benin (1970). Recommended high‑level manpower development.
Nigeria founded OAU (1963), AU (2002). Led anti‑apartheid movement (supported ANC, SWAPO). ECOMOG interventions: Liberia, Sierra Leone.
Nigeria pursued non‑alignment during Cold War. Maintained relations with both West and Eastern bloc. OPEC member (1971).
Sukur Cultural Landscape (Adamawa), Osun‑Osogbo Sacred Grove (Osun), Oban Hills/Korup (Cross River).
Durbar (Katsina, Kano, Sokoto – horse parade). Osun‑Osogbo (August). Argungu Fishing Festival (Kebbi). Calabar Carnival (December).
Chinua Achebe (‘Things Fall Apart’), Wole Soyinka (Nobel 1986), Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie (‘Half of a Yellow Sun’), Ben Okri (Booker 1991).
Second largest film industry in world by output. Started 1990s with video films. Major centres: Lagos, Enugu, Asaba.
Major religions: Islam (~50%), Christianity (~45%), Traditional (5%). Tensions in the Middle Belt (Jos riots, Boko Haram insurgency in North‑East).
English law, Customary law, Sharia law (12 Northern states). Supreme Court is highest. Court of Appeal has chambers in various zones.

STA 112.back: Mutually exclusive events: P(A∩B)=0, so P(A∪B)=P(A)+P(B). Complement: P(A')=1–P(A). Always check if events are mutually exclusive or independent before applying formulas.
P(B|A) reads ‘probability of B given A’. Independent: knowing A gives no info about B. Mutually exclusive (A∩B=∅) with positive probabilities are dependent (they cannot happen together).
Updates probabilities given new evidence. Denominator is total probability P(B). Partition space into mutually exclusive A₁, A₂, … Used in medical testing, spam filtering.
Mean affected by outliers. Median is robust. For grouped data: mean = Σfx/Σf, where x = class mark (midpoint). Class size = upper – lower limit.
σ²: population variance (divide by n). s²: sample variance (divide by n−1, Bessel’s correction). Range = max–min. IQR = Q3–Q1. Larger SD = more spread.
Conditions: fixed n, independent trials, two outcomes, constant p. nCk = n!/(k!(n−k)!). Example: number of heads in 10 coin flips.
Models rare events over time/space. λ = average rate. Approximation to binomial when n large, p small, np = λ. Mean equals variance – key property.
Symmetric, bell‑shaped. 68‑95‑99.7 rule: 68% within ±1σ, 95% ±2σ, 99.7% ±3σ. Standardise with Z‑score to use standard normal table. Mean=Median=Mode.

STA 112.formula: r=\frac{n\sum xy-\sum x\sum y}{\sqrt{[n\sum x^2-(\sum x)^2][n\sum y^2-(\sum y)^2]}}
STA 112.back: r ∈ [–1,1]. r=+1 perfect positive, r=–1 perfect negative, r=0 no linear relationship. r² (coefficient of determination) = proportion of variance explained.

STA 112.formula: P(A\cup B\cup C)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)-P(A\cap B)-P(A\cap C)-P(B\cap C)+P(A\cap B\cap C)
STA 112.back: Generalisation of inclusion‑exclusion. For mutually exclusive events, all intersection terms are zero.
Used for intersection of events. For independent events, P(A∩B)=P(A)P(B). Example: draw two cards without replacement.
Break event B into cases based on partition. Fundamental for Bayes’ theorem. Example: B = defective, Aᵢ = machine i.
Number of ways to arrange k distinct objects from n without repetition. Order matters. 0! = 1.
Number of ways to choose k items from n without regard to order. Used in binomial coefficient. Example: poker hands.
Number of ways to partition n distinct items into k groups of sizes n₁,…,nₖ. Example: anagrams of ‘MISSISSIPPI’.
Number of trials until first success. Memoryless: P(X>n+k|X>n) = P(X>k). Example: number of coin flips until first head.
Number of trials to get r successes. Generalises geometric (r=1). Example: number of tosses to get 3 heads.
Sampling without replacement from population of size N with K successes. Finite population correction. Example: drawing aces from a deck.
All intervals of equal length have same probability. Used for random number generation. Example: waiting time for a bus that arrives every 10 minutes.
Models waiting times. Memoryless: P(X>t+s|X>t)=P(X>s). Related to Poisson process (inter‑arrival times).
Generalises exponential (α=1). Γ(α) = ∫₀^∞ t^{α−1}e^{-t} dt. Sum of α iid exponentials. Example: waiting time for α events.
Special case of gamma (α=ν/2, β=1/2). Used in hypothesis testing (goodness‑of‑fit, variance estimation, independence).
Heavier tails than normal. Used for small‑sample inference on mean (unknown variance). Approaches N(0,1) as ν → ∞.
Used for ratio of variances (ANOVA, comparing two variances). Skewed, only positive. F = t² when ν₁=1.

STA 112.formula: \bar{X} \stackrel{approx}{\sim} N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right) \text{ for large } n, \text{ regardless of population distribution}
STA 112.back: Sum (or mean) of iid random variables tends to normality. Typically n ≥ 30 sufficient, but depends on population skewness.
Standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean. Estimated by s/√n when σ unknown. Decreases as √n.
z_{α/2} from standard normal. 95% CI uses z=1.96. Interpretation: 95% of such intervals contain μ.
Use t‑distribution with n−1 degrees of freedom. Wider than z‑interval because of additional uncertainty from estimating σ.
Requires n p̂ ≥ 10 and n(1−p̂) ≥ 10 for normal approximation. Wilson interval is more accurate for small n.
H₀: μ = μ₀. Compare |t| to t_{α/2, n‑1}. Reject H₀ if p‑value < α. Check assumptions: normality or large n.
Test H₀: p = p₀. Normal approximation, requires np₀ ≥ 10 and n(1−p₀) ≥ 10.

STA 112.front: Type I and Type II Errors
STA 112.formula: \alpha = P(\text{Reject } H_0 | H_0 \text{ true}),\quad \beta = P(\text{Fail to reject } H_0 | H_a \text{ true})
STA 112.back: α = significance level. Power = 1−β. Trade‑off: decreasing α increases β unless sample size increases.
β₀ = intercept, β₁ = slope. Least squares minimises Σ(yᵢ–ŷᵢ)². ŷ = b₀ + b₁x.
b₁ = sample covariance / sample variance of x. The regression line passes through (x̄, ȳ).
Proportion of variability in Y explained by X. In simple regression, R² = (r)². Higher R² better fit, but can be artificially high with many predictors.
Test equality of k group means. Under H₀, F ∼ F_{k−1, n−k}. Reject if F > critical value.
Pearson correlation on ranks. No normality assumption. dᵢ = difference between ranks of each pair.
Applies to any distribution with finite variance. For k=2, at most 25% of data beyond 2σ. Gives bounds even when distribution unknown.
Useful when only mean known. Weak bound but very general. Example: if average income is $50k, at most 10% earn ≥ $500k.
Moments from derivatives. Uniqueness: same MGF ⇒ same distribution. For sum of independent: M_{X+Y}(t)=M_X(t)M_Y(t).
ρ ∈ [−1,1]. Independent ⇒ ρ=0, but ρ=0 does not imply independence (only no linear relationship).
Population divided into strata, sample each. Reduces sampling error if strata are homogeneous. Allocates sample proportionally or optimally.
Smoothes fluctuations. Lag = (k−1)/2. For odd k, centre aligns with time points. Used to identify trends.
Sample mean converges to population mean. Weak LLN: convergence in probability. Strong LLN: almost sure convergence.

PHY-CM 104.back: Valid for constant acceleration only. s=displacement, u=initial velocity, v=final velocity, a=acceleration, t=time. For free fall: a = g = 9.8 m/s² (downward).
1st: object remains at rest/uniform motion unless acted on by net force. 2nd: F=ma (net force = mass × acceleration). 3rd: action–reaction pairs, equal and opposite. Units: N = kg·m/s².
Work‑Energy theorem: net work = change in KE. Conservation of mechanical energy (if no friction): KE+PE = constant. Power unit: Watt (W = J/s). Efficiency = (useful output)/(total input)×100%.

PHY-CM 104.formula: v=f\lambda,\quad T=\frac{1}{f},\quad v_{\text{sound}}\approx 340\text{ m/s},\quad v_{\text{light}}=3\times10^8\text{ m/s}
PHY-CM 104.back: v = speed, f = frequency (Hz), λ = wavelength (m), T = period (s). Transverse: vibration ⟂ propagation (light). Longitudinal: vibration ∥ propagation (sound).
Buoyant force = weight of displaced fluid (Archimedes). Object floats if its average density < fluid density. Bernoulli: faster flow → lower pressure. Pascal: pressure transmitted equally.
0th: thermal equilibrium (basis of temperature). 1st: energy conservation – ΔU = Q−W. 2nd: heat flows hot→cold spontaneously; entropy increases. 3rd: entropy→0 as T→0 K.

PHY-CM 104.formula: \text{Slope of } x\text{‑t = } v,\quad \text{slope of } v\text{‑t = } a,\quad \text{area under } v\text{‑t = displacement}
PHY-CM 104.back: Position–time: slope = instantaneous velocity. Velocity–time: slope = acceleration, area = change in position. Acceleration–time: area = change in velocity.
Velocity of A relative to B. Useful for boats crossing rivers, aircraft in wind. Example: boat velocity relative to water plus water velocity relative to ground gives boat relative to ground.

PHY-CM 104.formula: x = v_0 t,\quad y = h - \frac{1}{2}gt^2,\quad v_y = -gt,\quad v_x = v_0
PHY-CM 104.back: Independent motions: horizontal constant velocity, vertical free fall. Time of flight t = √(2h/g). Range = v₀√(2h/g).
Range R = v₀² sin(2θ)/g. Maximum height H = (v₀² sin²θ)/(2g). Maximum range at θ=45° (no air resistance).
Static friction opposes impending motion, up to μₛN. Kinetic friction acts during motion, constant μₖ. Usually μₛ > μₖ. N = normal force (often mg).
For ideal pulley (massless, frictionless), tension is same on both sides. Acceleration a = (m₂−m₁)g/(m₁+m₂). Tension = (2m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂))g.
Centripetal acceleration points toward centre. Centripetal force is net radial force. Examples: car rounding a curve, satellite orbit, ball on a string.
Bank angle θ for which no friction needed for a car to negotiate the curve at speed v. For higher speeds, friction must act inward.
Area under F‑x graph. For spring: F = –kx, W = ½k(x_i²–x_f²). Work done by spring is independent of path.
Torque τ (N·m) times angular velocity ω (rad/s). Works for engines, motors. Equivalent to P = Fv for linear motion.
Impulse equals change in momentum. For constant force: J = F Δt. Used in collisions, rocket propulsion.
Both momentum and kinetic energy conserved. For m₁=m₂, velocities swap. For stationary target v₂ᵢ=0, v₁f = ((m₁–m₂)/(m₁+m₂))v₁ᵢ.
Perfectly inelastic: objects stick together. Kinetic energy not conserved (lost as heat/sound). Momentum conserved.

PHY-CM 104.formula: \tau = rF_{\perp} = I\alpha,\quad I = \sum m_i r_i^2 = \int r^2 dm
PHY-CM 104.back: Newton’s second law for rotation. I depends on mass distribution. For a point mass m at distance r: I = m r². For solid sphere: I = 2/5 MR², disc: ½ MR².
For rolling object without slipping: total KE = ½mv² + ½Iω², with v = ωR (R = radius).
Angular momentum conserved if net external torque = 0. For point mass: L = r × p = m r v sinθ. Example: spinning ice skater pulling arms in (I decreases, ω increases).
Attractive force between point masses. Gravitational field g = GM/r² (for Earth, r ≥ R_earth). g on Earth’s surface ≈ 9.8 m/s².

PHY-CM 104.formula: \text{1st: Elliptical orbits, Sun at focus; 2nd: Equal areas in equal time; 3rd: } T^2 \propto a^3
PHY-CM 104.back: 3rd law: T² = (4π²/GM)a³ for circular orbit. a = semi‑major axis. For planets around Sun, T²/a³ = constant.
Minimum speed to escape gravitational field to infinity. From Earth: ~11.2 km/s. From Moon: ~2.4 km/s. Independent of direction (if no atmosphere).
Restoring force F = –kx. Velocity v = –Aω sin(ωt+φ), acceleration a = –ω²x. Energy: E = ½kA².
Period independent of mass. For large amplitude (>15°), period increases (error ~ (θ₀²/16)). Use small‑angle approximation sinθ ≈ θ.
I = moment of inertia about pivot, d = distance from pivot to COM. Generalises simple pendulum (I = m d² gives T = 2π√(d/g)).
Harmonics. Fundamental f₁ (first harmonic). Node at fixed ends, antinode at center for fundamental. For open/closed pipes, boundary conditions differ (open: antinode at open end).
Upper signs: observer moving toward source (increase f’) or source moving away from observer (decrease f’). v = speed of sound in medium (~343 m/s).
Intensity ∝ (amplitude)². Inverse square law for spherical waves: I ∝ 1/r². Doubling intensity increases decibels by 3 dB.

PHY-CM 104.formula: PV = nRT = NkT,\quad R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)},\quad k = 1.38\times10^{-23} \text{ J/K}
PHY-CM 104.back: n = number of moles, N = number of molecules. For fixed amount, P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂. Works for low‑density gases.
Average kinetic energy per molecule = (3/2)kT. v_rms = root‑mean‑square speed; M = molar mass (kg/mol).

PHY-CM 104.formula: Q = mc\Delta T,\quad Q = mL \text{ (phase change)}
PHY-CM 104.back: c = specific heat (J/(kg·K)). L = latent heat (fusion or vaporisation). Heat flows until thermal equilibrium. No temperature change during phase change.

PHY-CM 104.formula: \Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T,\quad \Delta V = \beta V_0 \Delta T,\quad \beta \approx 3\alpha
PHY-CM 104.back: α = coefficient of linear expansion (K⁻¹). For isotropic solids. β = coefficient of volume expansion. Example: railway tracks have gaps to allow expansion.
No heat exchange (Q=0). For monatomic gas γ=5/3, diatomic γ=7/5. Work done = –ΔU.
Second law: total entropy never decreases. For reversible isothermal: ΔS = Q_rev/T. Entropy is a state function.

PHY-CM 104.formula: Q = \frac{\pi r^4 \Delta P}{8\eta L},\quad F_{\text{visc}} = \eta A \frac{dv}{dy}
PHY-CM 104.back: Volumetric flow rate through cylindrical pipe. η = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s). Flow rate ∝ r⁴ – small radius greatly reduces flow.
γ (N/m) = force per unit length. Pressure excess inside a droplet (1 surface) = 2γ/r, inside a bubble (2 surfaces) = 4γ/r.
φ = work function (minimum energy to eject electron). Threshold frequency f₀ = φ/h. Electron emission only if f > f₀. Intensity affects current, not K_max.
Matter waves. For electrons, λ = h/√(2mE). Verified by electron diffraction. Macroscopic objects have negligible wavelength.

PHY-CM 102.formula: F=k\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2},\quad E=\frac{F}{q}=k\frac{Q}{r^2},\quad k=9\times10^9\text{ N m}^2\text{C}^{-2}
PHY-CM 102.back: Force between point charges, r separation. E = field due to source charge Q. Direction: away from +Q, toward –Q. ε₀ = 8.85×10⁻¹² C²/N·m²; k = 1/(4πε₀).
Field lines start at +ve, end at –ve, never cross. Density ∝ field strength. Add field vectors component‑wise: E_x = ΣE_ix, E_y = ΣE_iy.
Potential is scalar (easier than field). Work done moving charge q through ΔV: W = qΔV. Field points in direction of decreasing potential: E = –∇V.

PHY-CM 102.formula: Q=CV,\quad U=\tfrac{1}{2}CV^2=\tfrac{Q^2}{2C},\quad C_{\text{series}}^{-1}=\sum C_i^{-1},\quad C_{\text{parallel}}=\sum C_i
PHY-CM 102.back: C in Farads (F). Energy stored in electric field. Series: same Q, voltages add. Parallel: same V, charges add. Dielectric increases C: C = κC₀.

PHY-CM 102.formula: V=IR,\quad R=\frac{\rho L}{A},\quad R_{\text{series}}=\sum R_i,\quad R_{\text{parallel}}^{-1}=\sum R_i^{-1}
PHY-CM 102.back: ρ = resistivity (Ω·m), L = length, A = cross‑section. Series: same current, voltages add. Parallel: same voltage, currents add. Power: P = IV = I²R = V²/R.
Force on moving charge: right‑hand rule. Force on current‑carrying wire: F = BIL sinθ. Faraday: changing magnetic flux induces EMF. Lenz: EMF opposes change.

PHY-CM 102.front: Electric Field due to Continuous Charge
PHY-CM 102.back: Break distribution into infinitesimal dq. Linear λ (C/m), surface σ (C/m²), volume ρ (C/m³). Integrate component‑wise.
Relates net flux through closed surface to enclosed charge. Useful for symmetric distributions (spherical, cylindrical, planar).

PHY-CM 102.front: Field due to Infinite Line of Charge
PHY-CM 102.back: Radial field, magnitude ∝ 1/r. λ = linear charge density. Derived from Gauss’s law with cylindrical Gaussian surface.

PHY-CM 102.front: Field due to Infinite Sheet of Charge
PHY-CM 102.back: Uniform field independent of distance. For two oppositely charged sheets (capacitor): E = σ/ε₀ between plates, zero outside.
Scalar, easier than field integral. Then E = –∇V. For symmetric distributions, find V first, then differentiate to get E.
No work done moving charge on equipotential. Field lines point from high to low potential, perpendicular to equipotentials.
Total energy stored = ∫ u dV. For parallel‑plate capacitor: U = ½CV² = ε₀ E² × (volume).
Dielectric constant κ > 1 reduces E for same charge. χₑ = electric susceptibility, κ = 1+χₑ. Polarisation = dipole moment per volume.
α = temperature coefficient (positive for conductors, negative for semiconductors). For metals, resistance increases with T (higher lattice vibrations).
Sum of potential differences (including emfs and IR drops) = 0. Sign convention: voltage drop across resistor = –IR (going from + to –).
Charge conservation. Current entering equals current leaving. Used with KVL to solve complex circuits.
Time constant τ = RC. After τ, charge reaches 63% of final value. Final charge Q_f = Cℰ.
Initial charge Q₀. Current direction opposite to charging. After τ, charge reduces to 37%.

PHY-CM 102.front: Magnetic Field due to Long Straight Wire
PHY-CM 102.back: Right‑hand rule: thumb in current direction, fingers curl in B direction. Field lines circle the wire.

PHY-CM 102.front: Magnetic Field at Centre of a Loop
PHY-CM 102.formula: B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R} \text{ (loop)}, \quad B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R} n \text{ (solenoid, } n \text{ turns)}
PHY-CM 102.back: For N‑turn loop: multiply by N. For solenoid of length L with N turns: B = μ₀(N/L)I = μ₀nI, where n = turns per meter.
Used for high‑symmetry current distributions (wire, solenoid, toroid). Analogous to Gauss’s law for magnetism.

PHY-CM 102.front: Magnetic Force between Two Parallel Wires
PHY-CM 102.back: Attractive if currents same direction, repulsive opposite. Defines the ampere (attraction of 2×10⁻⁷ N/m for 1 A at 1 m).
Weber (Wb) = T·m². Flux through open surface. Faraday’s law uses rate of change of flux.
Determines direction of induced EMF. If flux through a loop increases, induced current creates B field opposing that increase.
For a rod of length ℓ moving perpendicular to B. Free charges experience magnetic force, creating potential difference across ends.
Unit: Henry (H). For solenoid: L = μ₀N²A/ℓ. Energy stored: U = ½Li².
Inductor opposes change. After τ, current reaches 63% of final value. Decay: i(t)=I₀e^{-t/τ}.
For sinusoids. Average power P_avg = V_rms I_rms cosφ (φ = phase angle between V and I).
Current leads voltage by 90° in capacitor. ω = 2πf. For DC (ω=0), X_C → ∞ (open circuit).
Voltage leads current by 90° in inductor. For DC, X_L=0 (short circuit).
Phase angle φ. At resonance (X_L = X_C), Z=R, φ=0, maximum current.
At resonance, circuit behaves resistively (current in phase with voltage). Used in tuning radios.
Maxwell’s equations predict transverse EM waves. In vacuum, speed c. E and B fields are perpendicular, in phase.
S = intensity (W/m²). Time‑average intensity I = c ε₀ E_rms² = (E_rms B_rms)/μ₀.
